questions.question: Explain the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation. Which memory allocation techniques cause each type?
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted memory within allocated block due to fixed-size units
External fragmentation: free memory scattered in non-contiguous blocks despite sufficient total space
Contiguous allocation causes external fragmentation
Paging causes internal fragmentation
Segmentation causes external fragmentation

questions.question: What is virtual memory? Explain how demand paging implements virtual memory.
keyPoints: Virtual memory extends physical memory using disk space
Processes can execute even if not fully in RAM
Demand paging loads pages only when referenced (page fault)
Page table tracks pages in memory vs disk
Reduces memory requirements and allows larger programs

questions.question: Describe the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm with an example. Also mention one advantage and one disadvantage.
keyPoints: SCAN moves head in one direction servicing all requests until end
Head then reverses direction to service remaining requests
Also called elevator algorithm
Advantage: reduced average seek time, services all requests fairly
Disadvantage: may cause longer wait for requests at extremities

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain the working set model as a solution to thrashing.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive paging causing CPU underutilization and poor performance
Occurs when processes don't have enough frames for their working set
Working set: set of pages actively used in a time window
OS maintains working set to ensure enough frames allocated
Prevents thrashing by keeping active pages in memory

questions.question: Explain the difference between paging and segmentation. Mention at least two advantages of each.
keyPoints: Paging: fixed-size pages/frames; Segmentation: variable-sized logical segments
Paging eliminates external fragmentation; Segmentation provides logical view
Paging causes internal fragmentation; Segmentation causes external
Paging advantage: simple memory management, no external fragmentation
Segmentation advantage: logical organization, easier code/data separation

questions.question: What is a page fault? Describe the steps taken by the OS when a page fault occurs.
keyPoints: Page fault: referenced page not present in memory
OS checks if reference is valid (if invalid, process terminates)
If valid, OS locates page on disk (swap space/file)
OS finds a free frame in physical memory
Reads page from disk into selected frame
Updates page table entry (sets valid bit, frame number)
Restarts the interrupted instruction

questions.question: Compare FCFS and SSTF disk scheduling algorithms with respect to throughput and fairness.
keyPoints: FCFS: services requests in arrival order; SSTF: services closest request first
SSTF generally has lower average seek time than FCFS
FCFS is fair - no request starved
SSTF may cause starvation of distant requests
FCFS throughput depends on request locations
SSTF more efficient but less fair than FCFS

questions.question: Explain the following page replacement algorithms: FIFO, Optimal, and LRU. For the following reference string, simulate each algorithm with 3 frames and calculate page faults: 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2
keyPoints: FIFO: replaces oldest page in memory
Optimal: replaces page not used for longest time in future
LRU: replaces least recently used page
Simulation shows optimal has minimum faults, FIFO maximum with given frames
FIFO faults: 9, LRU faults: 8, Optimal faults: 7 (with 3 frames)
Belady's anomaly possible in FIFO

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual memory and describe the various page replacement algorithms. Also discuss the working set model and its role in preventing thrashing.
keyPoints: Virtual memory: disk-based memory extension allowing larger programs
Demand paging loads pages on fault, lazy loading
Page replacement algorithms: FIFO, Optimal, LRU, Clock
FIFO simple but suffers Belady's anomaly
Optimal theoretically best but impractical
LRU approximates optimal, hardware support needed
Working set: pages actively used in recent time
Thrashing: excessive paging when working set not satisfied
Working set model allocates frames to maintain working set

questions.question: Discuss various disk scheduling algorithms. For a disk with 200 cylinders (0-199), and request queue order: 82, 170, 43, 140, 24, 16, 190, with initial head position at 50 moving toward higher cylinder numbers, calculate total head movement for FCFS, SSTF, and SCAN algorithms.
keyPoints: SSTF: always choose closest next - movement calculation shows shorter total
SCAN: move to end then reverse - calculates 50->199->0->190 = 149+199+190 = 538 (simplified)
SCAN (elevator) provides good performance, avoids starvation
C-SCAN offers uniform wait time, circular service
Each algorithm has different performance characteristics

questions.question: Explain the concept of segmentation with paging. How does it combine advantages of both techniques? Also explain how address translation works in such a system.
keyPoints: Segmentation: logical division into variable-sized segments (code, data, stack)
Paging: physical division into fixed-size pages/frames
Combined system: each segment divided into pages
Advantages: logical view (segmentation) + efficient memory use (paging)
Address translation: segment number -> segment table -> page table -> frame number
Virtual address: [segment number, page number, offset]
Physical address: [frame number, offset]
Eliminates external fragmentation from segments, reduces internal fragmentation impact
